content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660             322-5901            322-6441            322-6444


September 13, 1983


Thomas J. Harron
Assistant City Attorney
Civic Center
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA 92010

Re: Your Request for Advice, Our
Advice No. A-83-184

Dear Mr. Harron:

Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. You are
writing on behalf of Chula Vista City Councilmember David
Malcolm, Planning Commissioner William Cannon and Franklin
Gerdees, who hopes to be appointed to the City's Ethics
Committee.1/ The Ethics Committee provides advice and counsel
on the City's Code of ethics. Mr. Malcolm is a real estate
broker, and Messrs. Cannon and Gerdees are attorneys.

In your letter, you said that Messrs. Malcolm, Cannon and
Gerdees may, individually, be hired by developers to assist in
gaining the City's approval of proposed projects. The officials
will be paid on a contingent fee basis, receiving payment only
if a project is approved. As part of this employment agreement,
the broker and the attorneys will advise their employers on the
preparation and presentation of requests for such things as
conditional use permits, variances and tentative map approvals.
They may also contact persons living in the vicinity of a
project in order to address the neighbors' concerns and,
hopefully, lessen any opposition to the project. Your question
is whether the officials will violate the provisions of the
Political Reform Act by engaging in these activities.


1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code, unless otherwise indicated.

Thomas J. Harron
Septmeber 13, 1983
Page 2


DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 87100 provides that no local public
official can make, participate in the making of or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. An official has a "financial interest" in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to
the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made....

Government Code Section
87103(c).

In the present case, a developer hiring Messrs. Malcolm, Cannon
or Gerdees will become a source of income to the official once
$250 or more is received or promised.2/

If the result of a decision (such as a decision on whether
to grant a variance or a conditional use permit) will fore-
seeably have a material financial effect on either the project's
developer, or on the income of the official in question,3/ the
official is required to disqualify himself from "making,4/
participating, or in any way attempting to use his official
position to influence" the decision.


2/ A future contingent fee is "promised" income within
the meaning of the Act.

3/ (See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A).) The
approval or disapproval of a project can affect the official's
income because payment is on a contingency fee basis.

4/ You indicated that, under these circumstances, Messrs.
Malcolm and Cannon will disqualify themselves from "making" the
final decision regarding the approval or disapproval of a
request. It is unlikely that Mr. Gerdees, in his position on
the Ethics Commission, will be confronted with decision-making
of this nature.

Thomas J. Harron
Septmeber 13, 1983
Page 3


     In the following discussion, it is assumed that the effect
of a particular decision will be material.  Thus, it must be
determined whether the official may engage in the types of
activities that you have described.

     An official who engages in the activities described is not
making or participating in a decision, but he may be using his
official position to influence a governmental decision.  An
official uses his or her official position to influence a
decision by:

     ... furthering or attempting to affect in any manner
any decision:

          (1)  Within or before his or her agency; or

          (2)  Before any agency which is appointed by
or subject to the budgetary controls of his or her
agency.

                         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                         18700(e)(1), (2).

     Therefore, if a decision affecting a project, for which
an official is being paid a contingent fee, is before the
official's agency, or before one subject to his agency's
appointive or budgetary controls, the official in question
may not assist his employer, or his employer's representa-
tive, in the preparation or presentation of matters related
to the decision before the agency, nor may he represent or
act on behalf of his employer in discussions with members
of the public in connection with the preparation or
presentation of these matters.

     If the request is before an agency, other than those
described in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e)(1) and (2),
the official is free to engage in the conduct described.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to contact me at 916/322-5901.

                         Very truly yours,


                         [signature]
                         Janis Shank McLean
                         Counsel, Legal Division
JSM:km

City of Chula Vista, California

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

GEORGE D LINDBERG                                    THOMAS J HARRON
City Attorney                                        Assistant City Attorney

                                        July 25, 1983


Barbara Milman
FPPC
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA.  95804

Re:  Interpretation of California Administrative Code
     Section 18700

Dear Barbara:

This letter is a follow-up to our conversation of July 25, 1983
regarding the prohibitions in the Government Code on the use of
influence by City officials. (Government Code section 87100). As
I described to you, we have a real estate broker serving on the
City Council, an attorney serving on the Planning Commission, and
another attorney who wishes to serve on the Ethics Committee.
They have expressed concern as to whether they would be able to
continue with their normal employment activities by merely
abstaining on matters that they are interested in when they come
before them.

As you know, a public official may not "participate in the making
of a governmental decision" in which he or she has an interest.
California Administrative regulation 18700 (c)(1) states that this
participation includes negotiations, without significant substant-
ive review, with a private person regarding the decision. I am
told it is a typical business practice that real estate brokers
and attorneys will accept contingent fees, the contingency being
that a project is built. Oftentimes, in order for the project to
be built, the applicant will need to get some sort of City approval,
for example, a conditional use permit, a variance or tentative
map approval. The City official may advise the applicant on
strategy, arguments, etc. which will aid him in getting City
approval. The public official may also make efforts to resist
opposition to the project with neighbors by contacting them and


CIVIC CENTER • 276 FOURTH AVENUE • CHULA VISTA, CALIFORNIA 92010 • TELEPHONE (619) 691-5037

Barbara Milman
July 25, 1983
Page Two


addressing their concerns.  These activities could be carried out
without contacting City employees and the public official would,
of course, abstain when and if the decision on the project came
before him.

The question is whether these activities with the applicant and
other private persons in furtherance of the project constitute
participation in the making of a governmental decision.  If it
does, then the public official can not satisfy the requirements of
the Government Code by merely abstaining.  They would not be able
to enter into the contracts at all.  This may have a severe impact
upon their livelihood and their willingness to serve.

I would appreciate your consideration of this problem and either
an opinion letter or a published opinion so that I may inform these
public officials of their responsibilities under the Government
Code.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Thomas J. Harron
Acting City Attorney

TJH:lgk


P.S.  The cite to the Attorney General's opinion I mentioned
      concluding that a supervisor who is also a real estate
      broker may enter into a contingency agreement upon county
      approval of a use permit is 64 OPS. CAL. ATTY. GEN. 795
      (1981).

sections.question: Your question is whether the officials will violate the provisions of the Political Reform Act by engaging in these activities.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, if a decision affecting a project, for which an official is being paid a contingent fee, is before the official's agency, or before one subject to his agency's appointive or budgetary controls, the official in question may not assist his employer, or his employer's representative, in the preparation or presentation of matters related to the decision before the agency, nor may he represent or act on behalf of his employer in discussions with members of the public in connection with the preparation or presentation of these matters. If the request is before an agency, other than those described in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e) (1) and (2), the official is free to engage in the conduct described.

official can make, participate in the making, or in any way 
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 
An official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on:
sections.question_synthetic: Whether three Chula Vista city officials (a real estate broker serving as City Councilmember, an attorney on the Planning Commission, and an attorney seeking appointment to the Ethics Committee) may accept contingent fee employment from developers to assist with gaining city approval of projects, including advising on strategy and contacting neighbors, while abstaining from final decisions on those projects.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Officials who receive contingent fee compensation have a disqualifying financial interest under Government Code Section 87100 and cannot use their official position to influence decisions affecting the project or their income. They are prohibited from assisting employers in preparing or presenting matters to their own agency or agencies subject to their agency's control, but may do so for matters before other agencies.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter (July 25, 1983) and FPPC response letter (September 13, 1983). Extracted primary FPPC advice from response. Clear Q&C sections present. Some formatting issues with page breaks but content is intact.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether the officials will violate the provisions of the Political Reform Act by engaging in these activities.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, if a decision affecting a project, for which an official is being paid a contingent fee, is before the official's agency, or before one subject to his agency's appointive or budgetary controls, the official in question may not assist his employer, or his employer's representative, in the preparation or presentation of matters related to the decision before the agency, nor may he represent or act on behalf of his employer in discussions with members of the public in connection with the preparation or presentation of these matters. If the request is before an agency, other than those described in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e) (1) and (2), the official is free to engage in the conduct described.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Chula Vista city officials accepting contingent fee employment from developers to assist with city approvals have a disqualifying financial interest that prevents them from advising employers or addressing public concerns regarding projects before their own agency, though they may do so for matters before unrelated agencies.
